edge: Prototypical patches are used to recognize various components in an image in order to explain their reasoning to humans.
Prototype-based methods can learn prototypes that may not align with human visual perception, as the same prototype can refer to different concepts in the real world.
PIP-Net is introduced as an interpretable image classification model that learns prototypical parts in a self-supervised fashion which correlate better with human vision.
PIP-Net can be interpreted as a sparse scoring sheet where the presence of a prototypical part in an image adds evidence for a class.
PIP-Net with interpretable prototypes enables users to interpret the decision-making process in an intuitive, faithful, and semantically meaningful way.
Our prototypes correlate with ground-truth object parts, indicating that PIP-Net closes the 'semantic gap' between latent space and pixel space.

The reasoning of PIP-Net simplifies human identification of reasons for (mis)classification.

Both linear layer and training the model are essential components of the model optimization process. The linear layer influences the weights and prototypes, while training the model involves optimizing for classification performance.
The classification loss term LC affects the weights of the linear layer, thereby influencing the overall model performance.
The classification loss term LC not only influences the linear layer weights but also finetunes the prototypes for the downstream classification task.
One of the requirements for the interpretable classifier is that it should be explainable, which implies that the classifier's decision-making process should be transparent.
Another requirement for the interpretable classifier is that the explanations it provides should be compact, making the reasoning behind the predictions concise.
The interpretable classifier should be able to handle out-of-distribution data by either providing accurate predictions or abstaining from making a decision if the data is unfamiliar.
The custom activation function is designed to capture the objectives of the interpretable classifier, including explainability, compactness, and handling out-of-distribution data.
The linear classification layer is implemented as an interpretable scoring sheet that facilitates scoring-sheet reasoning, allowing the model to find evidence for multiple classes or none at all.
Neural networks involve scoring-sheet inference results as part of their output.
Scoring-sheet inference results are based on unnormalized output scores.
To convert unnormalized output scores to class confidence scores, a softmax activation function is applied during training.
Applying softmax activation function may conflict with the goals of compactness and decision abstaining in scoring-sheet reasoning.
Softmax is not scale-invariant, as it does not satisfy σ(z) ̸= σ(c · z) for scalar c.
The softmax output distribution depends on the class scores, resulting in skewed distributions for different scenarios.
When class scores are low, the softmax output tends towards a uniform distribution.
Having very high or very low softmax output scores can make the model susceptible to weight initialization issues and hinder effective training.
Softmax activation function is only applied during training to convert unnormalized logits to class confidence scores.
Softmax is only applied during training for interpretable scoring, not during inference.
Weight initialization plays a role in training stability and effectiveness.

Purity of CUB-prototypes w.r.t. object part annotations is quantified for different models including PIP-Net R.
The purity of CUB-prototypes is evaluated with respect to object part annotations. It calculates how often the same object part is present in the top-10 image patches per prototype.
The purity of prototypes is evaluated by selecting the top-10 image patches for each prototype and analyzing their encoding of the same object part.
The purity of PIP-Net prototypes after pretraining (without classification loss LC) is indicated. It refers to the prototypes before classification loss is applied.
For each part-prototype model, the 10 images with the highest similarity score for a particular prototype are selected to evaluate the encoding of object parts.
There is a correlation between the size of the explanation and the purity of prototypes for existing models. Larger explanations tend to have higher purity, in line with the 'sun OR dog' issue.
PIP-Net is described as compact and having pure, interpretable prototypes. It contrasts with existing models that may have issues with explanation size and purity.
PIP-Net with a ConvNeXt-tiny backbone achieves a substantially higher purity than other models.
PIP-Net with a ResNet-backbone achieves a higher purity than ProtoPNet, ProtoPShare, ProtoPool, and ProtoTree.
Even the self-supervised prototypes of PIP-Net-C have a higher purity score than the prototypes learned by other models with classification loss.
